---
layout: post
author: JuliaPoo
category: Mathematics
backend: typst
display-title: You Could've Discovered the Yoneda Lemma
tags:
- math
- category-theory
- yoneda-lemma
excerpt: An exposition on the Yoneda Lemma that's accessible to those without a math
  background. The Yoneda Lemma is a very useful statement in Category Theory which
  is usually communicated as 'you can completely understand something by observing
  how everything else relate to it'. It has been the subject of intense hype amongst
  pseudo-intellectual circles surrounding tech and AI, despite how, as we'll see in
  this post, the statement of the Yoneda Lemma is surprisingly dull.

---

To read this post as a (lightmode) pdf, [click here](/assets/posts/2026-06-19-yoneda-lemma-is-boring/2026-06-19-yoneda-lemma-is-boring.pdf).

<h2 id="loc-4">LK and LJ Calculi</h2>
<p>For the LK calculus, we follow the presentation in <a href="#loc-24" id="loc-5" role="doc-biblioref">[1]</a> but make a small adjustment. This’ll allow us to realise LJ as a restriction of LK as mentioned above. To keep LK symmetrical, I’ll also state rules for <span class="typst-eqn-inline"><svg class="typst-frame" height="7.513000000000001pt" style="overflow: visible; width: 0.778em; height: 0.683em;" viewbox="0 0 8.558 7.513000000000001" width="8.558pt" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" xmlns:h5="http://www.w3.org/1999/xhtml" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"><g><g class="typst-text" transform="matrix(1 0 0 -1 0 7.513000000000001)"><use fill="#ffffff" fill-rule="nonzero" x="0" xlink:href="#g9337EB6E93054F2383FFFD9233C8739" y="0"></use></g></g><defs id="glyph"><symbol id="g9337EB6E93054F2383FFFD9233C8739" overflow="visible"><path d="M 0 0m 7.678 2.97 h -6.798 c -0.176 0 -0.264 -0.07700014 -0.264 -0.22000003 c 0 -0.14300013 0.088 -0.22000003 0.264 -0.22000003 h 6.798 c 0.17600012 0 0.26399994 0.0769999 0.26399994 0.22000003 c 0 0.13199997 -0.13199997 0.22000003 -0.26399994 0.22000003 Z "></path></symbol></defs></svg></span> (co-implication/subtraction).</p>
<h3 id="loc-6">LK Calculus Formulation of Classical Logic</h3>

</div>
<p>The former is used to better connect LK and LJ calculus, as LJ calculus has restricted structural rules. This choice affects the <span class="typst-eqn-inline"><svg class="typst-frame" height="7.513000000000001pt" style="overflow: visible; width: 1em; height: 0.683em;" viewbox="0 0 11 7.513000000000001" width="11pt" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" xmlns:h5="http://www.w3.org/1999/xhtml" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"><g><g class="typst-text" transform="matrix(1 0 0 -1 0 7.513000000000001)"><use fill="#ffffff" fill-rule="nonzero" x="0" xlink:href="#g2D4E283C2551778A608A1D0A132DE7AB" y="0"></use></g></g><defs id="glyph"><symbol id="g2D4E283C2551778A608A1D0A132DE7AB" overflow="visible"><path d="M 0 0m 10.252 2.574 c 0.076999664 0.032999992 0.12100029 0.099000216 0.12100029 0.17600012 c 0 0.0769999 -0.044000626 0.14299989 -0.12100029 0.17599988 c -0.5279999 0.17600012 -1.0229998 0.5500002 -1.4849997 1.122 c -0.30800056 0.38500023 -0.5170002 0.83599997 -0.6160002 1.3530002 c -0.032999992 0.14300013 -0.12100029 0.20900011 -0.26399994 0.20900011 c -0.17600012 0 -0.26399994 -0.0880003 -0.26399994 -0.2750001 l 0.01099968 -0.021999836 v -0.011000156 c 0.18700027 -0.95700026 0.6710005 -1.7160001 1.474 -2.288 h -8.205999 c -0.176 0 -0.264 -0.08800006 -0.264 -0.26399994 c 0 -0.17600012 0.088 -0.26399994 0.264 -0.26399994 h 8.205999 c -0.8029995 -0.572 -1.2869997 -1.3310001 -1.474 -2.288 v -0.0109999925 l -0.01099968 -0.022000015 c 0 -0.18699999 0.08799982 -0.27499998 0.26399994 -0.27499998 c 0.14299965 0 0.23099995 0.066 0.26399994 0.20899999 c 0.09899998 0.517 0.3079996 0.96800005 0.6160002 1.353 c 0.4619999 0.5719999 0.9569998 0.946 1.4849997 1.1219999 Z "></path></symbol></defs></svg></span>L and <span class="typst-eqn-inline"><svg class="typst-frame" height="7.513000000000001pt" style="overflow: visible; </div>
<h3 id="loc-7">LJ Calculus Formulation of Intuitionistic Logic</h3>
<p>As mentioned previously, the LJ calculus is a restriction of LK calculus such that all sequences have exactly one formula on the right <span class="typst-eqn-inline"><svg class="typst-frame" height="7.513000000000001pt" style="overflow: visible; width: 2.5415555555555556em; height: 0.683em;" viewbox="0 0 27.957111111111114 7.513000000000001" width="27.957111111111114pt" This prohibits all right-structural rules and the <span class="typst-eqn-inline"><svg class="typst-frame" height="7.513000000000001pt" style="overflow: visible; width: 0.778em; height: 0.683em;" viewbox="0 0 8.558 7.513000000000001" width="8.558pt" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" xmlns:h5="http://www.w3.org/1999/xhtml" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"><g><g class="typst-text" transform="matrix(1 0 0 -1 0 7.513000000000001)"><use fill="#ffffff" fill-rule="nonzero" x="0" xlink:href="#g9337EB6E93054F2383FFFD9233C8739" y="0"></use></g></g><defs id="glyph"><symbol id="g9337EB6E93054F2383FFFD9233C8739" overflow="visible"><path d="M 0 0m 7.678 2.97 h -6.798 c -0.176 0 -0.264 -0.07700014 -0.264 -0.22000003 c 0 -0.14300013 0.088 -0.22000003 0.264 -0.22000003 h 6.798 c 0.17600012 0 0.26399994 0.0769999 0.26399994 0.22000003 c 0 0.13199997 -0.13199997 0.22000003 -0.26399994 0.22000003 Z "></path></symbol></defs></svg></span>R rule (hence I omit all <span class="typst-eqn-inline"><svg class="typst-frame" height="7.513000000000001pt" style="overflow: visible; width: 0.778em; height: 0.683em;" viewbox="0 0 8.558 7.513000000000001" width="8.558pt" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" xmlns:h5="http://www.w3.org/1999/xhtml" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"><g><g class="typst-text" transform="matrix(1 0 0 -1 0 7.513000000000001)"><use fill="#ffffff" fill-rule="nonzero" x="0" xlink:href="#g9337EB6E93054F2383FFFD9233C8739" y="0"></use></g></g><defs id="glyph"><symbol id="g9337EB6E93054F2383FFFD9233C8739" overflow="visible"><path d="M 0 0m 7.678 2.97 h -6.798 c -0.176 0 -0.264 -0.07700014 -0.264 -0.22000003 c 0 -0.14300013 0.088 -0.22000003 0.264 -0.22000003 h 6.798 c 0.17600012 0 0.26399994 0.0769999 0.26399994 0.22000003 c 0 0.13199997 -0.13199997 0.22000003 -0.26399994 0.22000003 Z "></path></symbol></defs></svg></span> rules in LJ). Explicitly, the derivation rules of LJ calculus is as follows:</p>

<h4 id="loc-10">Computational Content of LJ Calculus</h4>
<p><strong>Note</strong>: This entire section is just my intuition, it could be wrong in subtle ways</p>
<p>Since LJ calculus contain exactly one formula on the right, the unique <em>consumer</em> can be thought of as the ‘goal’ of the computation (or return value). Further, the producers can be interpreted as typed holes.</p>
<p>Since the consumer/goal is unique, we can build the proof-term (program) by annotating the goal. The ‘initialising’ of the holes can be done via a <code>let</code> statement. I’ll expound on this interpretation to establish a better connection between LJ and lambda calculus. Later, System L for LK calculus will be introduced as an extension of this idea.</p>
<p>Concretely, the computational interpretation is as follows:</p>
<div>
<div class="typst-eqn-block">

</div>
</div>
<p>Left-intro rules instantiate existing holes by destructing fresh holes while the right-intro rules construct terms. This is exactly the usual intro-elim rules in lambda calculus (or Gentzen’s NJ calculus). Hence, the LJ calculus can be seen as a type system for (polymorphic) lambda calculus.</p>
<p>In particular, each elimination rule can be derived by pairing a left-intro rule with a cut and eliminating the superfluous <code>let</code> statements:</p>
<div></div>
<p>More concretely, the NJ calculus can be described as follows:</p>
<p>TODO</p>
<p>NJ and LJ have equivalent proving power. I.e., a proof of NJ can be translated into LJ and vice-versa.</p>
<p>The difference between LJ calculus and NJ calculus is that LJ is explicit about the typed holes while NJ keeps the holes implicit. More concretely, using the substitution-based Krivine-style machine, NJ calculus has the following CBN operational semantics, which can be translated into an LJ proof-term, making the evaluation context explicit (only <span class="typst-eqn-inline"><svg class="typst-frame" height="7.513000000000001pt" style="overflow: visible; width: 1em; height: 0.683em;" viewbox="0 0 11 7.513000000000001" width="11pt" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" xmlns:h5="http://www.w3.org/1999/xhtml" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"><g><g class="typst-text" transform="matrix(1 0 0 -1 0 7.513000000000001)"><use fill="#ffffff" fill-rule="nonzero" x="0" xlink:href="#g2D4E283C2551778A608A1D0A132DE7AB" y="0"></use></g></g><defs id="glyph"><symbol id="g2D4E283C2551778A608A1D0A132DE7AB" overflow="visible"><path d="M 0 0m 10.252 2.574 c 0.076999664 0.032999992 0.12100029 0.099000216 0.12100029 0.17600012 c 0 0.0769999 -0.044000626 0.14299989 -0.12100029 0.17599988 c -0.5279999 0.17600012 -1.0229998 0.5500002 -1.4849997 1.122 c -0.30800056 0.38500023 -0.5170002 0.83599997 -0.6160002 1.3530002 c -0.032999992 0.14300013 -0.12100029 0.20900011 -0.26399994 0.20900011 c -0.17600012 0 -0.26399994 -0.0880003 -0.26399994 -0.2750001 l 0.01099968 -0.021999836 v -0.011000156 c 0.18700027 -0.95700026 0.6710005 -1.7160001 1.474 -2.288 h -8.205999 c -0.176 0 -0.264 -0.08800006 -0.264 -0.26399994 c 0 -0.17600012 0.088 -0.26399994 0.264 -0.26399994 h 8.205999 c -0.8029995 -0.572 -1.2869997 -1.3310001 -1.474 -2.288 v -0.0109999925 l -0.01099968 -0.022000015 c 0 -0.18699999 0.08799982 -0.27499998 0.26399994 -0.27499998 c 0.14299965 0 0.23099995 0.066 0.26399994 0.20899999 c 0.09899998 0.517 0.3079996 0.96800005 0.6160002 1.353 c 0.4619999 0.5719999 0.9569998 0.946 1.4849997 1.1219999 Z "></path></symbol></defs></svg></span> rules are shown):</p>

0.78540003 Z "></path></symbol></defs></svg></span> written above.</p>
<p>In particular, we could transform an NJ proof term by applying the translation above. The process is KINDA like a translation of NJ into LJ and <em>then</em> evaluating the LJ term via CBN semantics. Just that the two steps are interweaved. I don’t know what I’m talking about.</p>
<p>This seems similar to the vibe of <a href="#loc-25" id="loc-11" role="doc-biblioref">[2]</a> where perhaps LJ can be seen as a more explicit ‘intermediate language’.</p>
<h4 id="loc-12">Enforcing CBV evaluation strategy via CPS</h4>
<h3 id="loc-13">coLJ Calculus Formulation of co-Intuitionistic Logic</h3>
<h4 id="loc-14">Enforcing CBN evaluation strategy via co-CPS</h4>
<h4 id="loc-15">Computational Content of coLJ Calculus</h4>
<h3 id="loc-16">Wadler’s Duality</h3>
<h2 id="loc-17">Gentzen’s LJ and Closed Cartesian Categories</h2>
<p>We’ll focus on the propositional fragment of the LJ calculus.</p>
<p><strong>TODO: Expound on LJ without negation, BCC semantics, and then the problem of adding negation to motivate Pointed LJ</strong></p>
<p>For non-trivial computational semantics, we’ll be using a slightly modified definition of negation from the LJ calculus. In particular, we’ll define negation <span class="typst-eqn-inline"><svg class="typst-frame" height="7.513000000000001pt" style="overflow: visible; width: 6.082111111111112em; height: 0.683em;" The modified calculus, which we denote as pointed-LJ calculus, is concretely as follows:</p>
<div>
<p>Definition 1 (Pointed-LJ).</p>

Pointed-LJ can be seen as an extension of propositional LJ with a distinguished type <span class="typst-eqn-inline"><svg class="typst-frame" height="7.513000000000001pt" style="overflow: visible; width: 0.4640000000000001em; 0.1771 -0.18480015 0.1771 Z "></path></symbol></defs></svg></span> is intended to preserve non-degenerate computational semantics, a natural question is to ask if every LJ sequent admits a non-degenerate computational semantics as a pointed-LJ sequent. This question is intentionally left vague as the author does not have a good idea of what she’s writing about, but there’s a decently precise way to describe the negative result.</p>

<p>In order to highlight this distinction, we’ll change the notation of pointed-LJ slightly by denoting <span class="typst-eqn-inline"><svg class="typst-frame" height="7.513000000000001pt" <div>Note. Via cut elimination over proofs in LJ, the cut formula is actually not needed in the definition of the embedding. The above embedding being well-defined over proof reduction is the concern of the soundness theorem that’ll be discussed below.</div>
